title: 🚀 How Loavble Delivers Real-Time Code Previews Using Fly.io
author: Shantanupawar
text:
🚀 How Loavble Delivers Real-Time Code Previews Using Fly.io
One of the coolest things in Loavable is how the code instantly turns into a working live preview. You write some HTML or React, or the AI generates something for you, and almost immediately a running web project appears inside the browser preview.
This post is not the official architecture. It’s more like my own understanding of how it might be designed based on the behavior and interactions I’ve observed while building projects inside Loavable. The real implementation might differ, but here’s a pretty realistic look at what’s probably happening under the hood.
Why a System Like This Exists
Developers thrive when feedback is instant. Local tools like Vite and Next.js give Hot Module Reloading (HMR), letting changes appear in the browser within milliseconds. Doing the same thing in the cloud, for multiple users, requires:
• Separate dev environment per project
• Real-time updates without page refresh
• Ability to display inside an iframe
• Very fast startup times
• No “production deployments” per change
The goal is simple: a cloud dev environment that feels local.
Editing Starts in the Browser
The user writes code in an in-browser workbench. Every time a file changes, the change is temporarily queued with a small delay (about 150ms). This batching prevents unnecessary backend calls.
Once a batch is ready, the browser sends a request that triggers or updates the user’s own on-demand development machine in the cloud.
The Remote Dev Environment (Fly.io Machines)
Every live preview runs inside a lightweight Fly.io Machine:
• Node.js environment (example: Node 20 Alpine)
• 512 MB RAM, 1 CPU
• A persistent volume mounted at /workspace
• No public IP — only a private IPv6 address
What happens when a new preview environment is needed:
- A Fly machine is created through the Machines API
- A persistent volume is attached for the project
- Initial files are uploaded using Fly Exec API
- Dependencies are installed (npm/pnpm/yarn)
- A dev server is launched (Vite, Next.js, Remix, Astro, etc.)
- The backend registers this machine’s private IPv6 for routing
- The user receives a preview link like:
https://projectId.preview.example.app
Because files are on a persistent volume, restarts are fast and environments can restart efficiently after being paused.
Why a Router Service Sits in the Middle
Since the machines only have private IPv6 access, browsers cannot reach them directly. So there is a dedicated router application deployed on Fly.io that knows how to forward preview traffic.
When a browser requests the preview:
• The subdomain is parsed to extract the projectId
• The router looks up machine info (from an in-memory map or Redis later)
• The router proxies the HTTP request to the machine’s internal IPv6
• The machine’s dev server responds and assets are returned to the browser
This same proxy also handles WebSocket traffic, which is critical for HMR. Without it, live reload inside an iframe would instantly break.
How File Syncing Actually Works
Real-time file syncing here is not done over WebSockets. Instead, the system uses Fly Exec API over HTTPS.
When a file changes in the browser:
• Content is sent to backend
• File changes are encoded (Base64)
• Backend POSTs to the machine Exec API
• Machine runs something like:
mkdir -p /workspace/path && echo “<base64>” | base64 -d > file
• The dev server picks up the change
• HMR triggers automatically
Because updates are batched and rate-limited (for example: max 1 request per second), this avoids hitting Fly API rate limits.
WebSockets are only used for browser → router → machine HMR events, not file syncing.
Lifecycle Management and Cost Optimization
Machines should not run forever. So the system keeps track of:
• Last HTTP request timestamp
• Active WebSocket connections
• Recent file-sync activity
If everything goes idle for a while:
• Machine is stopped or deleted
• Volume remains, so progress is saved
When the user returns later, the environment can be spun back up quickly.
This balancing act keeps performance high while controlling cost.
A Quick End-To-End Data Flow
User writes or edits code
↓
Browser batches changes and sends them
↓
Backend creates or updates machine
↓
Files sync via Exec API
↓
Dev server updates → HMR triggers
↓
Preview iframe gets new UI instantly
Feels like running on localhost — but actually happening across a private cloud network.
Wrap-Up
This is one possible architecture behind the super fast live-preview systems emerging today. The exact setup may differ in some tools, but Fly.io Machines combined with a router proxy and file sync system is a highly realistic approach.
It’s pretty cool to think that every project in your browser might silently be running its own tiny VM somewhere in the world just to show you your latest code change a fraction of a second faster.
If you’re interested in more deep dives like this, feel free to tell me. I’d love to explore and document more of these modern cloud-development patterns.
